questions.question: Which security objective ensures that information is accessible only to authorized entities?
questions.description: Confidentiality prevents unauthorized disclosure of information. Encryption and access controls are commonly used to achieve it. Integrity protects against unauthorized modification, while availability ensures access when required.

questions.question: Which of the following is a symmetric encryption algorithm?
questions.description: AES is a symmetric block cipher that uses the same secret key for encryption and decryption. RSA, ECC and ECDSA are based on asymmetric cryptography.

questions.question: Which algorithm is least suitable for directly encrypting a very large file?
questions.description: RSA is computationally expensive and can process only limited-size input relative to its key and padding. Large files are normally encrypted using symmetric algorithms such as AES or ChaCha20.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes cryptographic hashing?
options: It converts fixed-size input into variable-size output
It is reversible using a public key
It converts arbitrary-size input into fixed-size output
It always requires a shared secret key
questions.description: A hash function accepts data of any practical size and produces a fixed-length digest. It is intended to be one-way and does not inherently require a secret key.

questions.question: A one-bit change in a message produces a drastically different hash. Which property is demonstrated?
questions.description: The avalanche effect means a very small input change causes a major, unpredictable change in the digest. This prevents visible relationships between similar inputs and outputs.

questions.question: Which hash algorithm is considered unsuitable for modern collision-resistant digital signatures?
questions.description: SHA-1 has demonstrated practical collision weaknesses and is deprecated for modern cryptographic signatures. SHA-2 and SHA-3 family algorithms are stronger alternatives.

questions.question: What does HMAC add to an ordinary cryptographic hash?
questions.description: HMAC combines a hash function with a shared secret. This provides integrity and message authentication because an attacker without the secret cannot generate a valid HMAC.

questions.question: Which security property is not normally provided by HMAC?
questions.description: HMAC does not encrypt the message, so the message may remain readable. It verifies integrity and proves that the sender possessed the shared secret.

questions.question: Which key is used to create a digital signature?
questions.description: The sender signs using the sender's private key. The recipient verifies the signature using the corresponding sender's public key.

questions.question: Which combination correctly represents confidentiality encryption between Alice and Bob?
options: Alice encrypts using Alice's private key; Bob decrypts using Alice's public key
Alice encrypts using Bob's public key; Bob decrypts using Bob's private key
Alice encrypts using Bob's private key; Bob decrypts using Alice's public key
Alice encrypts using Alice's public key; Bob decrypts using Bob's public key
questions.description: To protect confidentiality for Bob, Alice uses Bob's public key. Only Bob should possess the matching private key needed to recover the protected data.

questions.question: Which set of properties is most directly provided by a valid digital signature?
questions.description: A valid signature identifies the key holder, detects changes and provides evidence that supports non-repudiation. It does not by itself hide the signed message.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a digital certificate?
options: To bind a private key to a password
To bind an identity to a public key
To store a symmetric session key permanently
questions.description: An X.509 certificate associates an identified subject with a public key. A trusted CA digitally signs the certificate to support that binding.

questions.question: Which certificate field identifies the entity to which the certificate was issued?
questions.description: The Subject identifies the certificate holder. The Issuer identifies the CA that issued and signed the certificate.

questions.question: A browser connects to portal.example.com, but the server certificate contains only mail.example.com in its SAN. What is the most likely result?
options: The browser accepts it because both names use the same top-level domain
The browser reports a hostname mismatch
The browser ignores SAN and checks only the issuer
questions.description: The requested hostname must match an identity in the certificate's Subject Alternative Name extension. Having the same parent domain is insufficient.

questions.question: Which certificate configuration correctly identifies an end-entity TLS server certificate?
questions.description: A server certificate is not a CA, so Basic Constraints should indicate CA:FALSE. It commonly permits digital signatures and includes the serverAuth Extended Key Usage.

questions.question: Which extension determines whether a certificate may issue other certificates?
questions.description: Basic Constraints contains the CA:TRUE or CA:FALSE setting and may also include a path-length restriction.

questions.question: An Intermediate CA certificate contains Basic Constraints: CA:TRUE, pathlen:0. What does this mean?
options: It may issue end-entity certificates but not another subordinate CA
It may issue only Root CA certificates
It may issue unlimited subordinate CAs
questions.description: pathlen:0 prevents additional non-self-issued subordinate CA certificates below that CA. It may still issue end-entity certificates.

questions.question: Which sequence represents the standard hierarchical PKI trust model?
options: End Entity -> Root CA -> Intermediate CA
Intermediate CA -> End Entity -> Root CA
Root CA -> Intermediate CA -> End Entity
questions.description: The Root CA is the trust anchor. It signs an Intermediate CA, which then normally signs end-entity certificates.

questions.question: Why is a self-signed Root CA certificate trusted by a browser?
options: Because all self-signed certificates are automatically valid
Because its public key is secret
Because it is explicitly included in the browser or operating-system trust store
Because it has no expiration date
questions.description: Self-signing proves internal signature consistency, not trust. Trust exists because the relying system explicitly accepts the Root as a trust anchor.

questions.question: Which PKI component primarily verifies an applicant's identity before certificate issuance?
questions.description: The RA performs or coordinates identity verification and approves or rejects enrollment requests. The CA performs certificate issuance and signing.

questions.question: Which item is not normally included in a Certificate Signing Request?
questions.description: The CA assigns the serial number when it issues the certificate. A CSR contains the public key, subject information, requested extensions and the applicant's signature.

questions.question: Which PKCS standard defines the Certificate Signing Request format?
questions.description: PKCS#10 defines the standard structure for a CSR. It is one of the most frequently tested PKCS comparisons.

questions.question: Which format can normally carry a private key, its certificate and its certificate chain in one password-protected file?
questions.description: PKCS#12 files, commonly ending in .p12 or .pfx, may contain a private key, certificate and chain. PKCS#7 normally does not contain private keys.

questions.question: Which mapping is incorrect?
options: PKCS#11 - Interface for HSMs and cryptographic tokens
questions.description: PKCS#15 defines the organization of cryptographic data on smart cards and tokens. PKCS#10 defines CSR format.

questions.question: A security application must use an HSM-held signing key without exporting the private key. Which standard interface is most relevant?
questions.description: PKCS#11 defines an interface through which applications request cryptographic operations from HSMs, smart cards and tokens without directly retrieving private keys.

questions.question: What does FIPS 140-2 primarily evaluate?
options: The legal validity of electronic signatures
Cryptographic modules and their security controls
The strength of user passwords only
questions.description: FIPS 140-2 specifies security requirements for hardware and software cryptographic modules. It does not merely approve individual algorithms.

questions.question: A product uses AES-256, but the product itself has never undergone formal module validation. Which statement is most accurate?
options: The product is automatically FIPS 140-2 validated
AES use makes the complete product Level 4 compliant
The algorithm may be approved, but the module is not necessarily FIPS validated
FIPS validation applies only to certificates, not modules
questions.description: FIPS validation applies to a specific cryptographic module and tested configuration. Merely implementing an approved algorithm does not make a product validated.

questions.question: Which FIPS 140-2 level provides the highest degree of physical and environmental protection?
questions.description: Level 4 is the highest of the four FIPS 140-2 security levels. It adds extensive protection against physical and environmental attacks.

questions.question: What is the principal difference between certificate expiration and certificate revocation?
options: Expiration is planned termination; revocation invalidates a certificate before expiry
Revocation occurs only after the Not After date
Expiration requires CRL publication, while revocation does not
questions.description: Expiration occurs automatically when the validity period ends. Revocation is an explicit early termination caused by compromise, misissuance or another qualifying event.

questions.question: Which mechanism provides a periodically issued signed list of revoked certificate serial numbers?
questions.description: A Certificate Revocation List is generated and signed by a CA. It contains revoked certificate serial numbers and associated revocation information.

questions.question: Which statement correctly compares CRL and OCSP?
options: CRL checks one certificate; OCSP downloads all revoked certificates
CRL is always real-time; OCSP is always offline
CRL provides a signed list; OCSP provides status for an individual certificate
questions.description: CRLs are periodically published lists. OCSP allows a relying party to query one certificate and receive a signed status such as good, revoked or unknown.

questions.question: A web server obtains a signed certificate-status response from the CA and sends that response to browsers during the TLS handshake. Which mechanism is being used?
questions.description: With OCSP Stapling, the server retrieves, caches and presents the CA-signed OCSP response. This reduces browser latency, responder load and privacy leakage.

questions.question: During a modern TLS connection, which cryptography is normally used to protect bulk application data after the handshake?
questions.description: The TLS handshake establishes shared session keys. Efficient symmetric algorithms such as AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 then protect application data.

questions.question: In the cipher suite TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, what is the function of ECDHE?
questions.description: ECDHE establishes a temporary shared secret using elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman. RSA authenticates the server, AES-256-GCM protects data and SHA-384 supports cryptographic derivation or handshake processing.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of Perfect Forward Secrecy?
options: It allows reuse of the same session key forever
Compromise of a long-term private key does not reveal previously recorded sessions
It eliminates the need for authentication
questions.description: Ephemeral DHE or ECDHE keys create a fresh session secret for each connection. Later compromise of the server certificate key should not expose past sessions.

questions.question: A user enters a password and then another independently chosen password. How should this authentication be classified?
questions.description: Both passwords are something the user knows. MFA requires factors from different categories, such as a password and a physical token.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes OAuth from OpenID Connect?
options: OAuth provides authentication, while OIDC only encrypts tokens
OAuth provides delegated authorization, while OIDC adds authentication and identity
questions.description: OAuth answers what an application may access. OIDC adds an identity layer and answers who the authenticated user is.

questions.question: Which authentication protocol uses a Key Distribution Center and encrypted tickets?
questions.description: Kerberos uses a trusted KDC to issue tickets and session keys. It avoids repeatedly transmitting user passwords to every network service.

questions.question: An employee authenticates to enterprise Wi-Fi through a network access server that forwards the request to a central system performing authentication, authorization and accounting. Which protocol is most directly involved?
questions.description: RADIUS centralizes AAA for network access and is widely used with Wi-Fi controllers, VPN gateways, switches and network-access systems.

questions.question: A company adopts the following rules: no user is trusted merely because they are on the internal network; every access request evaluates identity and device condition; users receive only the minimum required permissions; security teams design controls assuming an attacker may already be present. Which model is being implemented?
questions.description: Zero Trust is based on explicit verification, least privilege and assuming breach. Network location by itself is not treated as proof of trust.
